name: Statistics & Probability
description: Master the **statistical foundations** essential for data science and machine learning — distributions, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, correlation, and Bayesian thinking.

questions.text: What does the **central limit theorem** state?

choices.text: The distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the population distribution
The mean of a dataset is always in the centre of its distribution
Large datasets always follow a normal distribution
The variance of a sample always equals the variance of the population

questions.explanation:
The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that if you take sufficiently large random samples from any population (with finite mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$), the distribution of sample means $\bar{X}$ will be approximately:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

This is why the normal distribution appears so frequently in statistics and enables many statistical tests.

questions.text: In hypothesis testing, what is a **p-value**?

choices.text: The probability of observing a result at least as extreme as the one obtained, assuming the null hypothesis is true
The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability that the result is practically significant
The threshold used to decide statistical significance

questions.explanation:
The **p-value** is $P(\text{data} \geq \text{observed} \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$.

Common misconceptions:
- ❌ p-value ≠ P(H₀ is true)
- ❌ 1 − p-value ≠ P(H₁ is true)

If p < α (significance level, often 0.05), we reject H₀. This threshold α is decided *before* the test, not by the p-value itself.

questions.text: What is the difference between **Type I** and **Type II** errors?

choices.text: Type I: false positive (reject true H₀); Type II: false negative (fail to reject false H₀)
Type I: false negative; Type II: false positive
Type I errors only occur with small samples; Type II errors only with large samples
They are the same error described from different perspectives

questions.explanation:
| Error | Also called | What happened |
|-------|------------|---------------|
| **Type I** (α) | False positive | Rejected H₀ when it was actually true |
| **Type II** (β) | False negative | Failed to reject H₀ when it was actually false |

**Statistical power** = 1 − β = probability of correctly detecting a real effect.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about **correlation** are correct? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Correlation measures the linear relationship between two variables
Correlation ranges from −1 to +1
Correlation implies causation
A correlation of 0 means the variables are statistically independent

questions.explanation:
Key points about (Pearson) correlation $r$:

- $r \in [-1, 1]$: −1 = perfect negative linear, 0 = no linear, +1 = perfect positive linear
- Measures **linear** relationships only — $r = 0$ doesn't mean no relationship (could be non-linear)
- **Correlation ≠ causation**: ice cream sales and drowning rates are correlated (both caused by hot weather)
- Independence implies $r = 0$, but $r = 0$ does not imply independence

questions.text: What is a **95% confidence interval** for a population mean?

choices.text: An interval constructed so that 95% of such intervals, built from repeated samples, would contain the true mean
An interval that has a 95% probability of containing the true population mean
An interval containing 95% of the data points
An interval 95% of the time centred on the sample mean

questions.explanation:
The frequentist interpretation: if we repeated the sampling process many times and computed a 95% CI each time, **95% of those intervals** would contain the true parameter.

The common misconception is: "this interval has a 95% probability of containing the true mean." In frequentist statistics, the true mean is a fixed (non-random) quantity — it either is or isn't in any given interval.

questions.text: What does **Bayes' theorem** allow us to compute?

choices.text: The posterior probability P(H|E) from the prior P(H), likelihood P(E|H), and evidence P(E)
The probability of two independent events occurring together
The expected value of a continuous distribution
The correlation between two Bayesian networks

questions.explanation:
**Bayes' theorem**:

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

| Term | Name | Meaning |
|------|------|--------|
| $P(H)$ | Prior | Belief before seeing evidence |
| $P(E \mid H)$ | Likelihood | Probability of evidence given hypothesis |
| $P(E)$ | Marginal | Total probability of evidence |
| $P(H \mid E)$ | Posterior | Updated belief after evidence |

Foundation of Bayesian statistics and spam filters (Naive Bayes).

questions.text: What is the symbol commonly used for the **standard deviation** of a population?

choices.text: σ
sigma

questions.explanation:
**σ** (Greek letter sigma) is the standard deviation of a population.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\sigma$ | Population standard deviation |
| $s$ | Sample standard deviation |
| $\sigma^2$ | Population variance |
| $s^2$ | Sample variance |

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$

questions.text: What is the difference between **mean**, **median**, and **mode**?

choices.text: Mean is the average; median is the middle value; mode is the most frequent value
Mean, median, and mode are all the same for any dataset
Mean is least affected by outliers; median is most affected
Median is the average of all values; mode is the middle value

questions.explanation:
| Measure | Definition | Robust to outliers? |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Mean** | Sum / count | No |
| **Median** | Middle value (50th percentile) | Yes |
| **Mode** | Most frequent value | Yes |

For skewed distributions (e.g., income data), the median is often more informative than the mean.

questions.text: In your own words, explain the concept of **statistical power** and what factors influence it.
questions.explanation:
**Statistical power** (1 − β) is the probability that a test correctly rejects a false null hypothesis — i.e., detects a real effect when one exists.

**Factors that increase power:**
- Larger sample size
- Larger true effect size
- Higher significance level α (but this increases Type I errors)
- Lower variability in the data
- Using a one-tailed instead of two-tailed test (when appropriate)

Underpowered studies are more likely to produce false negatives and non-replicable results.

questions.text: Which probability distribution would you use to model the **number of successes** in a fixed number of independent Bernoulli trials?

choices.text: Binomial distribution
Poisson distribution
Normal distribution
Exponential distribution

questions.explanation:
The **Binomial distribution** $B(n, p)$ models the number of successes in $n$ independent trials, each with probability $p$:

$$P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$$

| Distribution | Models |
|-------------|-------|
| Binomial | # successes in n trials |
| Poisson | # events in fixed time/area |
| Normal | Continuous symmetric outcomes |
| Exponential | Time between events |
